coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dot anchored 1-D WO3 for enhanced solar water splitting: A nano surface imaging evidence of charge separation and accumulation

coredata.dc:description: Combining WO3 with suitable materials to form heterojunction is essential to overcome the limitations of WO3 to enhance its photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting activity. Moreover, a clear understanding of photo-response and charge behavior of materials could lead to the rational design of efficient photoelectrodes. Given this, an efficient strategy is applied to fabricate WO3 heterojunction with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and in-depth characterization to investigate the surface charge dynamics using nano imaging in a relation to the enhanced PEC water splitting activity. The optimized NCDs loading to the WO3 NRs exhibited the enhanced photocurrent density of 1.54 mA cm−2 at 1.23V vs RHE under AM 1.5 G illumination, highest IPCE of ~82 % (at 308.32 nm). The Kelvin probe force microscopy and electrostatic force microscopy reveal that after loading NCDs to the WO3, a relatively smooth charge transport has been observed, which improves the PEC. Furthermore, this work demonstrates the effect of photogenerated charges caused by the NCDs that assist in enhancing the increased photocurrent, hydrogen production efficiency, and stability of the PEC water splitting system. Significantly, the nano imaging characterization utilized in this work could be extended to various photoanodes to study the surface charge dynamics.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The step-wise fabrication scheme of WO3-NRs and NCDs deposition to obtain WO3 NRs/NCDs photoelectrode.
(a) Low- and (b) high-resolution TEM images with (c) d-spacing values (0.34 nm) of NCDs. (d) AFM image of NCDs with its (e) height profile presentation.

(a) Low and (b) high resolution TEM images of WO3 NR. (c) Low and (d-f) high resolution TEM images of WO3 NR/NCDs. (g) STEM images of WO3 NR/NCDs showing the selected area for elemental mapping. (h) UV–Vis absorption spectra of WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs. Inset in (h) shows magnified spectra from the selected wavelength range (450–500 nm) indicating a clear change in absorption. (i) Tauc's plot of WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs.

(a) LSV and (b) photoconversion efficiency of WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs measured as a function of applied potential. (c) IPCE spectra WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs measured at an applied bias potential of 1.23 V vs RHE. (d) Nyquist plots under AM 1.5 G illumination were obtained at 1.23 V RHE in 0.5 M Na2SO4.

(a) MS plot of WO3 NRs and (b) WO3 NRs/NCDs. (c) XPS valence band-edge spectra of WO3 NRs. (d) HOMO-LUMO potential position determination by using cyclic voltammetry in 0.5 M Na2SO4. (e) Band edge diagram of WO3 NRs and NCDs heterojunction indicating charge generation, separation and transportation during the illumination to carry out PEC water splitting reaction. (f) hydrogen gas evolution of WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs samples, measured at 1.28V in a 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte under AM 1.5 G irradiation.

Surface topography image obtained by KPFM measurements for bare WO3 (at 1 μm scale for all the samples) (a) under dark and (b) illumination condition and for WO3 NRs/NCDs (c) under dark (d) under illumination. Local probe force microscopy measurements showing static EFM maps corresponding to different V tip at −2.0 V for (e) WO3 NRs and (g) WO3 NRs/NCDs. Phase change as a function of V tip under dark and light illumination conditions for (f) WO3 NRs and (h) WO3 NRs/NCDs.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dot anchored 1-D WO3 for enhanced solar water splitting: A nano surface imaging evidence of charge separation and accumulation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy Systems Research, Ajou University, Suwon 16499, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Energy Systems Research, Ajou University, Suwon 16499, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science & Engineering, Ajou University, Suwon 16499, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science & Engineering, Ajou University, Suwon 16499, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Energy Systems Research, Ajou University, Suwon 16499, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Combining WO3 with suitable materials to form heterojunction is essential to overcome the limitations of WO3 to enhance its photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting activity. Moreover, a clear understanding of photo-response and charge behavior of materials could lead to the rational design of efficient photoelectrodes. Given this, an efficient strategy is applied to fabricate WO3 heterojunction with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and in-depth characterization to investigate the surface charge dynamics using nano imaging in a relation to the enhanced PEC water splitting activity. The optimized NCDs loading to the WO3 NRs exhibited the enhanced photocurrent density of 1.54 mA cm−2 at 1.23V vs RHE under AM 1.5 G illumination, highest IPCE of ~82 % (at 308.32 nm). The Kelvin probe force microscopy and electrostatic force microscopy reveal that after loading NCDs to the WO3, a relatively smooth charge transport has been observed, which improves the PEC. Furthermore, this work demonstrates the effect of photogenerated charges caused by the NCDs that assist in enhancing the increased photocurrent, hydrogen production efficiency, and stability of the PEC water splitting system. Significantly, the nano imaging characterization utilized in this work could be extended to various photoanodes to study the surface charge dynamics.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen fuel could play a key role in future sustainable energy systems as demand for energy is globally increasing. Moreover, an environment friendly energy resources are in urgent need to replace the current fossil fuel energy systems. Among the available energy sources, hydrogen possesses significantly high energy density compared to conventional fossil fuels and hence emerges as one of the ideal replacements to fossil fuels [1–5]. Furthermore, hydrogen could prominently become a sustainable and renewable energy source with carbon-free and zero greenhouse gas emissions. Given this, the photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting has become one of the essential and rapidly developing research areas due to its renewable and sustainable route of producing hydrogen and oxygen. The PEC water splitting enables the production of hydrogen using semiconductor photoelectrode by the direct conversion of abundant resources like solar energy and water. The first experiment of hydrogen and oxygen production using solar water splitting technique was carried out in the early 70s, using TiO2 semiconductor [6]. Since then, significant efforts have been made to carry out PEC hydrogen production using different materials.

Among various semiconductor materials, metal oxides are particularly considered as an important class of photoelectrode material for the direct conversion of solar to hydrogen energy [7,8]. The important factors in selecting a suitable material as photoelectrode for water splitting are the optical band gap, stability during illumination, and, importantly, the position of valence and conduction bands with respect to water oxidation/reduction potentials. Furthermore, the photoelectrode must have the optical bandgap higher than the thermodynamic potential energy of 1.23 eV to drive the water splitting reaction [9]. In view of this, numerous metal oxide materials, including TiO2 [10], BiVO4 [11], ZnO [12], and WO3 [13–17], CdS [18] have been employed as PEC photoelectrodes via the improvement in chemical stability, charge separation, and mobility of charge carriers [19]. Specifically, coating the oxide photoelectrodes using the co-catalyst strategies (such as Pt, Au, RuOX, CoPi, NiooH) is reported to be the most effective strategy to enhance reactions kinetics and stability of the PEC water splitting reaction [20]. Among the metal oxides, WO3 is considered as the most critical semiconductor material having promising PEC water splitting capabilities due to its small bandgap (of 2.5–2.8 eV) and hole diffusion length of (~150 nm) [21]. Being an n-type semiconductor, WO3 possesses high electron hall mobility (12 cm2V−1s−1) with the absorption of 12 % of the solar spectrum [22,23]. Moreover, WO3 can exhibit its highest theoretically calculated photo-conversion to H2 efficiency of 4.8 % [24].

Recently considerable efforts have been made to improve the PEC water splitting efficiency of WO3 electrode. However, the experimental water-splitting efficiency of WO3 is reported to be limited due to inefficient utilization of incident light and insufficient charge transport at the interface of electrode and electrolyte and stability [25]. To overcome the aforementioned issues of WO3, different strategies can be implemented including, the formation of heterojunction [26], doping [21], controlling the morphology and crystallographic orientation [27] and by enhancing the light absorption by reducing the surface reflection [28]. For example doping of Bi to WO3, enhances the PEC water splitting properties by altering its bulk and surface properties [29]. Furthermore, the crystallographic orientation of WO3 could be tuned to increase the PEC performance via controlling synthesis or doping strategy [30,31]. Among these, the construction of the heterojunction of WO3 with suitable materials is known to be an effective strategy for enhancing its PEC water splitting activities [32]. Carbon dots (CDs) consisting of few atomic layers offer unique, excellent optical and electrical properties, which allow them to be utilized in many important applications, including solar water splitting [33]. Due to the presence of negligible surface defects, the CDs offer excellent electron transfer between the interfaces and grain boundaries when attached to various metal oxide materials [34]. Additionally, the small size of carbon dots provides a larger effective surface area for electrochemical reactions. In view of this, the CDs are generally sensitized with metal oxides to construct heterojunction systems for efficient water splitting applications [35,36]. The PEC water splitting properties of CDs can be further increased by doping with nitrogen atoms, which tends to improve the electrode-electrolyte interface and charge transport in the heterojunction [37]. Because the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) exhibit improved catalytic, electric and chemical properties compared to undoped CDs [38] and thus could be employed efficiently in PEC water splitting applications by compositing with various materials [39,40].

Based on the limitation of WO3 and the advantages of NCDs, the surface sensitization of WO3 using NCDs could be an effective strategy to enhance the PEC water splitting activity. By adopting the WO3/NCDs heterojunction system strategy, major technical challenges such as the extension of solar absorption capability, the suppression of charge recombination, and the improvement of charge transfer resistance at electrode/electrolyte interfaces could be resolved. Since NCDs have been extensively utilized as a sensitizer for various photoanodes, a clear understanding of the surface carrier characteristics of the electrode during the illumination is essential to understand the effect of NCDs as a sensitizer. Because the surface of the photoelectrode dictates the recombination or charge transfer for water splitting reaction and the trapped charges or states could easily affect the overall efficiency. Therefore, in addition to the detailed electrochemical characterization, the surface atomic force microscopy (AFM) studies could be required to clearly understand the photogenerated charge dynamics. To analyze the surface charge carrier's behavior, the kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) and phase mode electrostatic force microscopy (EFM) was used. Using the EFM measurements, the topographical and electrical mapping can be performed by scanning the tip of AFM over the surface of the sample. Based on the surface homogeneity, which is dictated by the trapped surface charges, the electrostatic force experienced by the tip onto surface changes leads to the change in the phase. Hence, mathematically, the shift of phase can be calculated that could allow a clear understanding of photogenerated charge carriers' behavior at the surface during the PEC water splitting.

Given this, here we propose the utilization of WO3 NRs/NCDs heterojunction for the enhanced PEC, hydrogen production activity, and stability. The WO3 nanorods were synthesized using the hydrothermal method, and NCDs were sensitized using the drop-casting method. The crystal structure, morphology, optical, and charge transport properties of heterojunction were evaluated. The conduction band minimum, valence band maximum, and bandgap of prepared heterojunction is analyzed by using spectroscopic and electrochemical techniques. The optimized concentration of NCDs with WO3 NRs shows a photocurrent density of 1.54 mA cm−2 at 1.23V vs RHE under 1.5G illumination. Furthermore, the photogenerated carrier dynamics was monitored by surface imaging KPFM and EFM, confirming that the presence of anchored NCDs significantly increases the charge carriers on the surface of WO3 NRs and improves the charge separation to decrease the recombination.

Ammonium paratungstate, urea (Mw = 60.02), sodium sulfate anhydrous, hydrochloric acid and hydrogen peroxide were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Citric acid anhydrous was purchased from Samcheon chemical (South Korea). Ultrapure water was (Water point company, South Korea) was used throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of tungsten oxide nanorods (WO3 NRs)
ce:section.ce:para: WO3 NRs were synthesized using the reported method [41] with slight modifications. For the synthesis, 1g of ammonium paratungstate {(NH4)10(H2W12O42)·4H2O} was dissolved in 100 ml of deionized water with strong stirring, and 2.2 ml of concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl) was added to this viscous solution and kept for vigorous stirring for 1 h. The solution becomes transparent and converts to yellow precipitate during 1 h of stirring. Hydrogen peroxide (5.5 ml) was added to this solution to dissolve the tungstic acid and kept for 2 h of stirring, and then the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, (filling 65 %). The FTO substrate (which was subsequently cleaned using acetone, isopropanol and deionized water) was placed in the autoclave with its conductive side facing down. The autoclave was kept at a temperature of 150 °C for 4 h. After the synthesis of WO3 NRs, the autoclave was allowed to cool down at room temperature, then the sample was cleaned with deionized water and annealed at 500 °C for 2 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and WO3 NRs/NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots were synthesized using the reported method with slight modification [42]. In the typical synthesis, 0.15 g of citric acid and 0.20 g of urea were dissolved in 10 ml of DI water and stirred for 30–50 min to obtain a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, and the reaction was carried out at a temperature of 150 °C for 5 h. The autoclave was cooled down to room temperature and the synthesized carbon dots were centrifuged several times with ethanol and deionized water to remove the unreacted chemicals. The yield product obtained after centrifuge was in small quantity (0.15 mg/ml). To synthesize WO3 NRs/NCDs, the NCDs was drop-casted onto WO3 thin films. The optimized sample was obtained by loading different amounts of NCDs, on the WO3 NRs samples and were heated to 80 °C for 1 h.

The structural analysis of the prepared sample was done by using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ultima III (USA) diffractometer). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on Theta Probe Base System of Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). The structural analysis was performed on field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) of Hitachi S-4800 (Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using JEOL system JEM-2100F (Japan). The size of nitrogen-doped carbon dots was estimated by using atomic force microscope (NT-MDT). All electrochemical measurements were recorded employing a potentiometer (Ivium technologies Ivium-n-stat system, Netherland). UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out by using a Varian Carry 5000 spectrophotometer instrument. ABET Technologies Sun 2000 solar simulator (ABET Technologies, USA) calibrated to AM 1.5G irradiance conditions was used for the light irradiance. The incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurement was carried out using a 1000 W xenon arc lamp (Thermo Oriel 66902) combined with a monochromator (Thermo Oriel Cornerstone 7400 1/8). The PEC measurements were performed in a quartz cell with a Pt wire counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode in 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1.

Local electrical properties were investigated with ex-situ AFM (NT-MDT) by using conductive Pt/Ir-coated Si probes (AC240-TM) having a curvature radius of approximately 30 nm, stiffness of approximately 2 Nm−1, and resonance frequency of approximately 70 kHz. In addition, the Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) and electrostatic force microscopy (EFM) measurements were performed in the tapping mode at a user-defined height of 40 nm. All measurements were performed in air. It may be mentioned that a large number of places were randomly chosen to check the uniformity in the results and the measurements were performed under different scan rates (ranging from 0.2 to 1 Hz). However, no noticeable change was observed in the results, confirming the absence of any tip-related artefact. The schematic of the nanoscale electrical measurements under light illuminations was shown in Fig. S11 (supporting information). In addition, the intensity of illumination source was fixed at 1.4 mW cm−2.

The fabrication scheme of WO3 NRs/NCDs heterojunction photoelectrode is depicted in Fig. 1 . WO3 NRs were grown on FTO substrate by hydrothermal method. As synthesized NCDs dispersed in ethanol solution were drop casted on the FTO/WO3 NRs and annealed at 80 °C for 1 h. After the drop-casting of NCDs on WO3 NRs, the thin film color changes to slightly dark brownish, confirming the deposition of carbon dots (CDs). The density of NCDs in ethanol was calculated to be 0.15 mg/ml. Different amounts of NCDs i.e 100 μl, 300 μl, and 500 μl were loaded on FTO/WO3 NRs. Note that the PEC properties of FTO/WO3 NRs loaded with different amounts of NCDs were investigated and the optimum amount that shows maximum photocurrent was chosen for the detailed characterization. The size and morphology of as-synthesized NCDs were shown in low and high-resolution TEM images (Fig. 2 (a–c)). The average size of synthesized NCDs was appeared to be ~3 nm having a spherical shape and found to be well dispersed in the solution as shown in Fig. 2(a and b). The lattice spacing of NCDs was calculated to be 0.34 nm ascribed to the (002) crystal facet of pure graphite (Fig. 2c). The AFM images of NCDs presented in Fig. 2(d and e) show that the topographical height of the NCDs is between 1 and 2.5 nm. This indicated the presence of multilayers in NCDs instead of a single layer with an average size of 2.5 ± 0.5 nm.

The optical properties of NCDs was studied using UV–Vis and PL spectroscopic techniques, as depicted in Fig. 3 . The strong absorption that appeared in the region of 200–300 nm (Fig. 3a) was attributed to the
π −
π ∗
transitions in the sp2 domains. Note that such transitions are commonly found in all graphene-based materials [43,44]. An additional absorption shoulder was noticed 420–481 nm (Fig. 3a) attributed to the transition of electrons from the n-orbital arising from the presence of several functional groups at the edge of NCDs (i.e –OH group which are weak electron donor, C–N and CO bonds) [45]. Based on the transmission spectrum of NCDs, the optical band gap was calculated to be 2.21 eV as presented in Fig. S1 (supporting information). The PL spectra shown in Fig. 3b was obtained via the exciton at a wavelength of 325 nm, which produces two emissions at 430 nm (E1) and 520 nm (E2). The E1 emission observed in the NCDs PL spectra was ascribed to the
π −
π ∗
transitions of the sp2 bonded carbon atoms and
n −
π ∗
transitions of C – O functional groups present in the NCDs. The E2 emission observed at 520 nm was found to be appeared due to the N doping, which gives rise to the C – N functional group in NCDs and hence induces a defect in the synthesized NCDs. The surface electronic states and composition of NCDs was investigated using XPS as depicted in Fig. 3. The XPS survey spectrum showed the presence of C1s, N1s, and O1s (Fig. 3c) centered at 284.7 eV, 400.01eV, and 531.48 eV, respectively, indicating the successful doping of N into carbon dots. The weight percent of (obtained from XPS analysis) nitrogen was found to be 16.81% in carbon dots. After the deconvolution, the high-resolution C1s spectrum shows three peaks at binding energy values of 287.58 eV, 285.25 eV, and 284.63 eV belongs to CN/CO, C–N, and CC/C–C functional groups, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3d [46]. The deconvolution of high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 3e) showed peaks at binding energy values of 399.11, 400.21 and 405.46 eV corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively [47]. The presence of these functional groups indicates the successful doping of N inside the carbon structure of carbon dots. Furthermore, the presence of functional groups of CO, C–OH/C–O–C, and COOH was observed at 531.12, 531.92, and 533.05 eV of binding energy values, respectively from the deconvolution of O1s peak (Fig. 3f), confirming the formation of NCDs [48]. TEM measurements were performed to analyze the morphology of WO3 NRs/NCDs as shown in Fig. 4 . The low and high-resolution TEM images of only WO3 NRs are presented in Fig. 4a, b. The TEM images of WO3 NRs/NCDs heterojunction at different resolutions are shown in Fig. 4c-f. In the TEM images of WO3 NRs/NCDs, the NCDs decorated on the surface of WO3 NRs were clearly seen, which were highlighted by yellow lines. The TEM images show that, the deposition of a few layers of NCDs on the surface of NRs. Fig. S2 (Supporting Information) shows the additional high-resolution TEM images confirming the presence of NCDs on the surface of WO3 NRs. Note that d-spacing was also calculated by taking average of 10 fringes at various locations of WO3 NRs. The d-spacing value was calculated to be 0.369 nm representing {200} plane of monoclinic WO3 as shown in Fig. 4f. To further confirm the presence of NCDs on the surface WO3 NRs, the scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) along with mapping was performed as presented in Fig. 4g and Fig. S3 (Supporting Information). The mapping images demonstrate the presence of N and C only at the area where NCDs were present, confirming the effective deposition of NCDs on WO3 structure. Furthermore, the STEM image with the elemental mapping of only NCDs was given in Fig. S4 (Supporting Information). The quantitative amounts of C and N in the NCDs were found to be 84.01%and 15.99 % respectively. Moreover, the EDS analysis also performed for WO3 NRs samples (Fig. S5) show the absence of C peak.

The UV–Vis spectroscopic measurement was carried out to analyze the change in optical bandgap properties of WO3 NRs before and after NCDs deposition. The absorption spectra of bare WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs heterojunction shown in Fig. 4h indicate a slight red shift in absorbance onset of WO3 NRs after the NCDs deposition as shown in the inset of Fig. 4h. The Tauc's plot was used to calculate the optical band gap using the following Eq. (1) [49].
(1)
α h v = A
(
h v −
E g
)

where α is the absorption coefficient, h is the Planck's constant,
v
represents the frequency A is the absorbance,
h v
and Eg represents the photon energy and band gap, respectively. The bandgap was determined by plotting (αhν)1/2 vs. hν as shown in Fig. 4i. The bandgap of bare WO3 NRs was calculated to be 2.41eV and found to be decreased to 2.28eV after the WO3 NRs/NCDs formation indicating the extended absorption of incident light compared to bare WO3.

XRD measurements were carried out to check the crystalline nature of the synthesized materials. The XRD pattern of bare WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs heterojunction is shown in Fig. S6 (Supporting Information). The XRD pattern reveals that the as-prepared WO3 NRs exhibit a monoclinic crystal structure (JCPDS card no. 752072). However, the XRD pattern of WO3 NRs/NCDs heterojunction did not show any significant change even after the NCDs deposition. This indicates that the NCDs were present on the surface of WO3 NRs without causing any significant change in the monoclinic structure of WO3 NRs. The surface electronic states and the composition of the synthesized photoelectrode was analyzed by using XPS spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. S7 (Supporting Information). The XPS spectrum of W4f peak upon deconvolution shows two major peaks at binding energy value of 37.45eV and 35.42eV corresponding to W4f5/2 and W4f7/2, respectively. The presence of W4f5/2 and W4f7/2 indicated that the WO3 NRs dominantly contain +6 oxidation state and is in good agreement with the previously reported results [50]. Further deconvolution of the peaks revealed the presence of a low quantity of +5 oxidation state of WO3 by the appearance of peaks at a binding energy of 33.88eV and 36.45 eV.

The PEC properties of the WO3 NRs before and after the NCDs was analyzed by linear sweeping voltammetry (LSV) under 1.5 G simulated solar light irradiation (100 mW cm−2) in 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. The LSV plots of bare WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs in the dark and under irradiation of light are shown in Fig. 5 a. The bare WO3 NRs photoelectrode exhibited the photocurrent density of 0.56 mA cm−2 at 1.23V vs. RHE which was found to be increased to 1.54 mA cm−2 (at 1.23V vs. RHE) after the NCDs deposition. Note that the photocurrent tripled due to the presence of NCDs on WO3 NRs. The optimum amount of NCDs required to produce maximum photocurrent was evaluated by drop-casting different amounts of NCDs. The photocurrent density of WO3 NRs increased with the increase in the loading amount of CDS, as shown in Fig. S8 (Supporting Information). A gradual increase in the photocurrent was observed with the increase in NCDs drop casting amount up to 500 μl. Further increase in NCDs drop casting amount above 500 μl was found to decrease the photocurrent as shown in Fig. S8. Because, the thick layer of NCDs (above 500 μl deposition) could delay electron injection process from NCDs to WO3 causing poor charge separation and increased charge recombination [51]. Furthermore, the increased amount of NCDs on WO3 could block the electrolyte penetration and effective contact between electrode and electrolyte [51]. Hence, NCDs on WO3 deposition with 500 μl solution was selected as the optimum quantity for effective heterojunction fabrication. The photo-conversion efficiency (photon to hydrogen efficiency) of bare WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs was calculated by using the below Eq. (2) [52]:

where η represent the photo-conversion efficiency, I shows the photocurrent density (mA cm−2), Jlight represents the irradiance of incident light (mW cm−2), E°rev represents the standard reversible potential (1.23 V vs. RHE), and the applied bias potential vs. RHE is represented by V. The photo-conversion efficiency of bare WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs is shown in Fig. 5b. The highest photo-conversion efficiencies of WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs was calculated to be 0.24 % and 0.96 % at 0.71 V vs RHE, respectively. That is, upon NCDs deposition the photo-conversion efficiency of WO3 increases nearly 4 folds. Furthermore, the incident photon-to-current efficiencies (IPCE) at 1.23V vs RHE was obtained as a function of incident light wavelength for WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs as shown in Fig. 5c using the following Eq. (3) [53].

where I represent the photocurrent density, λ is the wavelength of incident light and Jlight shows the measured irradiance. The highest IPCE illustrates that the WO3 NRs/NCDs showed enhancement in photoactivity as compared to WO3 NRs of 300–500 nm. Particularly, the photoactivity of WO3 NRs/NCDs was pronounced in the region of 300–450. Overall, the IPCE results indicate the significant contribution from NCDs for increased incident light absorption. The electrochemical impedance measurements were carried out to investigate the charge-transfer resistance between electrode and electrolyte interface. The Nyquist plots of WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs against 1.23V vs RHE is shown in Fig. 5d. The semicircle region presented by each of the electrode could be used to evaluate the charge transfer resistance between electrode and electrolyte. The WO3 NRs/NCDs show a smaller semicircle region compared to WO3 NRs and hence confirm the low charge transfer resistance at the electrode and electrolyte interface. This indicates that the holes produced during the irradiation could be transferred to electrolyte very efficiently via NCDs interface.

To understand the alteration in flat band potential at the interface of WO3 NRs photoelectrode, the Mott-Schottky (MS) plot was obtained before and after the NCDs deposition. The MS measurements were carried out at the frequency of 1 kHz in the absence of light using the following Eq. (4) [18].

where C defines the charge capacitance, Vs represents applied potential, ND indicates the electron carrier density, ε 0 is for the permittivity of the vacuum, ε is the relative permittivity of the semiconductor, e shows the elementary charge, T is the temperature (298 K) and kB is for the Boltzmann constant. The plot of 1/C2 vs the applied potential for WO3 NRs is shown in Fig. 6 a. The positive slope indicating n-type characteristics before NCDs deposition with electrons as a majority charge carrier. The value of flat-band potential was determined from the intercept of MS plots and found to be 0.61 V vs RHE for WO3 NRs.The MS plot of WO3, after the NCDs depositon is shown in Fig. 6b, the flat band potential of WO3 NRs/NCDs was noticed to be shifted cathodically compared to the bare WO3 after the NCDs deposition indicating the conduction band edge position above that of WO3. This ensures effective electron transfer from the NCDs to WO3 during the illumination, likewise hole transfer from WO3 valence band to NCDs implying effective charge separation and suppression of recombination.The XPS valence band analysis was utilized to investigate the energy gap between the Fermi level and the valence band edge as shown in Fig. 6c. The energy difference between the Fermi level and the valence band edge was calculated to be ~1.93eV for WO3 NRs.

Generally, the flat band potential of n-type semiconductors is considered to be situated more positively against their conduction band edge positions. That is, the conduction band edge could possibly be located at ~0.1 eV above the flat band potential [19]. Based on these assumptions, we predict that the conduction band edge positions (vs RHE) of 0.51 V for WO3 NRs and 0.45 V for the WO3 NRs NCDs. This indicated that the formation of heterojunction between WO3 and NCDs causes the shift in Fermi level alightment resulting in upward shift in conduction band edge after the heterounction formation. Due to very thin layer depostion of the NCDs on the WO3, a drastic change in conduciton band is not expected. Hence, for the clear understanding, the band edge position of NCDs were evaluated by calculating the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). The HOMO-LUMO positions were determined using cyclic voltammetry (CV) as shown in Fig. 6d. NCDs were drop casted on glassy carbon electrode for CV measurments in 0.5 M Na2SO4. Based on the oxidation and reduction peaks, the bandgap was calculated to be 2.2 eV, which is in agreement with the optical band gap of NCDs. Importantly, the HOMO and LUMO positions were found to be 2.17eV and −0.04eV vs RHE, respectively. Clearly, the LUMO of NCDs was positioned above the CB of WO3, confirming that upon light illumination, electrons produced at NCDs will be effectively transferred to the WO3 NRs Based on the band edge positions, optical band gap values, and the Fermi level positions, the probable band edge positions of WO3 NRs/NCDs heterojunction and NCDs was constructed as shown in Fig. 6e. Based on the band edge diagram, the relationship between the optical properties of the heterojunction and the PEC performance are evaluated. That is, when the WO3 NRs/NCDs absorbs the energy of the incident photon equal to or higher than the optical band gap, excitation of electrons from the VB to CB inWO3 NRs/NCDs and HOMO to LUMO in NCDs takes place, producing electron-hole pairs individually. Due to the favorable band edge positions, the photoexcited electrons at NCDs shifts from LUMO to the CB of WO3 NRs, which are transferred to counter electrode to carry out hydrogen production reaction and at the same time, the hole created at the VB of WO3 NRs/NCDs will be transferred to HOMO of NCDs and then to the electrolyte to carry out oxidation reaction. Furthermore, the hydrogen production capability of WO3 NRs/NCDs was tested in 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte under simulated AM 1.5 solar irradiation. The amount of hydrogen gas produced (~0.04 m mol/cm2 for WO3 NRs/NCDs) under the simulated solar radiation for 5 h is shown in Fig. 6f. As in the figure, the hydrogen production efficiency of WO3 NRs nearly doubled after the NCDs incorporation. The I-t curves obtained during the hydrogen production is represented in Fig. S9.

The observed increase in the photocurrent of WO3 NRs/NCDs electrode compared to bare WO3 NRs was attributed to several modifications caused by the sensitization of NCDs. Note that WO3 NRs/NCDs show appreciable increase in the IPCE values in the wavelength range of 300–400 nm which was ascribed to the absorption due to the existence of C and N states (CN bond) that contribute to the π∗ orbital allowing the absorption of energy equivalent to ~3.1 eV.Whereas the presence of O state enables the absorption of ~3.64 eV via the transition of n→π∗ transition due to the CO bond in the NCDs [42]. Furthermore, the narrow bandgap of NCDs compared to WO3 redshifts the absorption limit in the heterojunction. Importantly, the enhanced photoactivity above 500 nm of incident irradiation due to the n−π∗ transition enables the extended utilization of incident light [42].

To understand the underlying charge transfer and light-matter interaction mechanism, KPFM and EFM measurements were carried out for WO3 NRs before and after the NCDs deposition. The KPFM was performed to visualize the charge transfer and carrier separation as shown in Fig. 7 (a)–(d). Fig. 7a and (b) exhibit the contact potential difference (CPD) maps of only WO3 NRs under dark and white light illumination, respectively. On the other hand, the CPD maps of NCDs/WO3 NRs are shown in Fig. 7c and (d), respectively. The corresponding topography images of KPFM maps and EFM maps are shown in Fig. S10 (supporting information). Interestingly, KPFM results showed that the presence of NCDs on WO3 NRs decreases the CPD value in dark and under white light illumination conditions as compared to bare WO3 NRs. The decrease in the CPD values reveals that the significant increase in photogenerated charge carriers on the surface of WO3 NRs in the presence of anchored NCDs and thus, the NCDs/WO3 NRs heterostructure provides relatively smooth charge transport. However, KPFM is suitable for CPD mapping and photovoltage images but to build a detailed analysis on the charge transport under illumination, could be difficult because of several charge recombination processes. To address these issues, EFM measurements were performed, which yields the surface potential and dynamics of photoinduced charge transport [30,54]. The EFM-phase mode technique is typically used to study electrical properties of semiconductor by the utilization of gradient of force, which is electrostatic force between the sample surface and the AFM probe. The EFM technique is a dual-pass technique; in the first scan, the surface topography in the tapping mode was recorded and in the second scan, the conductive AFM tip is lifted at a constant height (z) from the surface and followed the previously recorded topography. In addition, a fixed voltage is applied to the tip with respect to sample surface which induces an opposite charge on the sample surface and the EFM phase signal is recorded. Thus, the electrostatic force is proportional to the gradient of capacitance and can be expressed by the following Eq. (5) [54].
(5)

where F(z) is the electrostatic force, C is the capacitance between tip and sample, U is the potential difference between the sample surface and tip of AFM, V tip is the tip potential, and V surface is the surface potential of the sample. The EFM-phase mode mapping of WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs at −2.0 V tip voltage under the dark conditions are shown in Fig. 7 (e, g). A clear phase contrast was observed between the WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs samples because of the electrostatic force between the tip and sample surface. The charge mapping of WO3 NRs showed the presence of lower charge (electrons) concentration. However, charge mapping for the WO3 NRs/NCDs sample appears smooth and exhibited more charge accumulation on the surface, indicating that NCDs provided electrons to WO3 NRs. To analyze the quantitative information of photogenerated charge carriers, we determined the phase change as a function of tip voltage as depicted in Fig. 7 (f, h) for WO3 NRs and WO3 NRs/NCDs samples, respectively. As the phase change is directly related to the potential difference between the tip potential and sample surface, a symmetric parabola was observed as shown in Fig. 7f when the Vtip = Vsurface. It is interesting to note that there is no change in the phase and minima of the parabola under dark and light illumination (Fig. 7f). On the other hand, in the case of WO3 NRs/NCDs sample, under light illumination, the minima of parabola was changed to 0.23V, as indicated by vertical dotted lines in Fig. 7h. This indicates the improvement in the charge transport and the photogenerated charge carriers are separated by the build in potential at the interface of WO3 NRs and NCDs.

The observed nanoscale results match well the obtained impedance spectra (Fig. 5d). Thus, the overall results indicate that the presence of NCDs, allows facile charge transfer at the electrode/electrolyte interface, offering low resistance to carry out water splitting reaction compared to bare WO3 NRs. Furthermore, the formation of NCDs heterojunction with WO3 is more effective in achieving better charge separation by suppressing recombination and to improve the PEC activity [26]. Therefore, the enhanced hydrogen production efficiency of WO3 NRs/NCDsheterojunction-water splitting was ascribed to the favorable optical and surface properties offered by the NCDs on the surface of WO3 NRs.

In summary, WO3 NRs/NCDs heterojunction was fabricated by simple hydrothermal synthesis followed by the drop-casting method. Characterization results indicated that the synthesized NCDs appear to have ~3 nm of its size with uniform distribution on WO3 NRs. The optical measurements showed the increased capability of light absorption of WO3 NRs/NCDs compared to bare WO3. The optimized amount of NCDs loading on WO3 NRs produced the highest photocurrent density of 1.54 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs RHE under AM 1.5 G illumination. Importantly, IPCE results showed increased light absorption in the UV region (300–400 nm) as well as extended absorbance in visible light range (500 nm–600 nm) for WO3 NRs/NCDs heterojunction contributing to the increased PEC activity. Furthermore, KPFM and EFM-phase mode technique was employed to image the change in charge concentration at the WO3 surface before and after the NCDs deposition that support the enhanced PEC activity of the device. Overall, the results showed that the optimized NCDs-loaded WO3 NRs can be utilized for stable, increased, and efficient PEC water splitting to hydrogen production.
